question_title: How many Atlantic Tropical Weather Outlooks issued by the NHC between June 8-14, 2026 will mention an area of disturbed weather in the Gulf of Mexico with a nonzero formation chance?
summary:
This question asks how many of the 28 scheduled NHC Atlantic Tropical Weather Outlooks during June 8-14, 2026 will mention a Gulf of Mexico disturbance with nonzero formation chance. The distribution is zero-inflated: ~59% probability of 0 (no Gulf disturbance flagged), with ~41% chance of some nonzero count driven by model signals for Central American Gyre development around June 13-15.

Current NHC outlook (June 5) says "no formation expected in next 7 days," strongly suppressing early-window activity. However, multiple numerical models (ECMWF, GFS, Google AI) converge on potential Gulf development around June 13-15 via a Central American Gyre pattern. ECMWF ensemble gives 35-50% probability of TD-level circulation by June 15; Dr. Ryan Maue gives >50%. If development materializes, NHC would likely start flagging it in TWOs during the last 2-3 days of the window (June 12-14), yielding ~4-12 qualifying TWOs.

The 2026 season is forecast below-normal (El Niño developing), which provides a mild headwind. The median outcome is 0, but the mean is ~3.2 due to the right tail when development does occur.

factors.description: NHC 7-day outlook (June 5) says 'no formation expected' through ~June 12, covering most of the window
Multiple numerical models (ECMWF, GFS, Google AI) converging on Central American Gyre development in Gulf around June 13-15
ECMWF ensemble gives 35-50% probability of TD-level circulation by June 15; Dr. Maue gives >50% by June 15
Historical base rate: NHC flagged Gulf disturbances during June 8-14 in 4/11 years (36%)
2026 season predicted below-normal (El Niño developing) with enhanced wind shear
Development timing is late in window (June 13-15) so even if disturbance forms, only last 2-3 days would count

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question:** How many Atlantic TWOs (out of 28 max) will mention a Gulf of Mexico disturbance with nonzero formation chance during June 8-14, 2026?

### Key Findings

| Percentile | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| P10 | 0 |
| P25 | 0 |
| **P50 (median)** | **0** |
| P75 | 6 |
| P90 | 11 |
| P95 | 14 |

**Mean: ~3.2 | Mode: 0**

### Distribution Shape
This is a **zero-inflated distribution** with two main scenarios:

1. **~59% probability: 0 TWOs** — Nothing develops in the Gulf during the window. This is supported by the NHC's current 7-day outlook ("no formation expected") and the below-normal season forecast.

2. **~41% probability: 1-28 TWOs** — The Central American Gyre pattern flagged by ECMWF, GFS, and Google AI models materializes, causing NHC to flag a Gulf disturbance in the last 2-3 days of the window. Most likely count in this scenario: **4-12 TWOs** (conditional median: 7).

### Key Evidence
- **NHC (June 5):** "Tropical cyclone formation is not expected during the next 7 days" — strong suppression of early-window activity
- **Model convergence:** ECMWF ensemble (35-50% for TD by June 15), GFS, Google AI all flag potential Gulf development via Central American Gyre
- **Dr. Ryan Maue:** >50% probability of TS/Hurricane in Gulf by June 15
- **Historical base rate:** 4/11 years (36%) saw Gulf mentions during this exact week
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) How many Atlantic Tropical Weather Outlooks issued by the NHC between June 8-14, 2026 will mention an area of disturbed weather in the Gulf of Mexico with a nonzero formation chance?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43783)

[Chances growing for June tropical development in the Gulf. Here’s the latest](https://www.clickorlando.com/weather/2026/06/05/chances-growing-for-june-tropical-development-in-the-gulf-heres-the-latest/)

[European and Google Models Signal Potential Tropical Development Over the Gulf of Mexico by Mid June 2026 as Hurricane Season Monitoring Intensifies - Saluda Standard Sentinel](https://saludastandard-sentinel.com/european-and-google-models-signal-potential-tropical-development-over-the-gulf-of-mexico-by-mid-june-2026-as-hurricane-season-monitoring-intensifies/)

[GFS and Euro Models Both Signal Potential Tropical System Development in the Gulf by June 5 Raising Early Awareness for the Gulf Coast as Long Range Uncertainty Remains High - Saluda Standard Sentinel](https://saludastandard-sentinel.com/gfs-and-euro-models-both-signal-potential-tropical-system-development-in-the-gulf-by-june-5-raising-early-awareness-for-the-gulf-coast-as-long-range-uncertainty-remains-high/)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves as the count of scheduled Atlantic Tropical Weather Outlooks (TWOs) issued by the U.S. National Hurricane Center with an issuance timestamp on or after 00:00 EDT on June 8, 2026 and before 00:00 EDT on June 15, 2026 (i.e., the four daily scheduled outlooks each calendar day from June 8 through June 14 inclusive, Eastern Time) that explicitly identify at least one area of disturbed weather located wholly or partly in the Gulf of Mexico (also referred to by NOAA as the "Gulf of America") and assign that area a nonzero 2-day or 7-day formation chance (i.e., any value other than "near 0 percent" / 0%).

A TWO counts if the text body describes a disturbance, low, tropical wave, or area of low pressure whose described location includes any portion of the Gulf of Mexico / Gulf of America (including embedded systems described as being over Florida, the northwestern Caribbean adjacent to the Yucatán, or coastal Mexico/U.S. Gulf coast when the text states the system is in or expected to move into the Gulf), AND that area is assigned a formation chance greater than 0% over either 2 days or 7 days. TWOs that say "Tropical cyclone formation is not expected during the next 7 days" or that flag disturbances only outside the Gulf do not count.

The primary resolution source is the NHC text TWO archive at https://www.nhc.noaa.gov/archive/2026/ (Atlantic Tropical Weather Outlook product), with the graphical archive at https://www.nhc.noaa.gov/archive/xgtwo/gtwo_archive.php as confirmation. Enter the count as an integer (e.g., a value of 12 outlooks resolves as "12").
fine_print: Only the four scheduled Atlantic TWOs per day (issued around 2 AM, 8 AM, 2 PM, and 8 PM EDT) are counted. Special/intermediate TWOs issued off-cycle are not counted. If a scheduled TWO is missed/not issued, it simply does not contribute to the count. Each qualifying TWO contributes 1 to the count regardless of how many Gulf disturbances it lists. If the NHC archive is temporarily unavailable, the resolver may rely on mirrored copies via [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) or the Iowa State IEM text archive. NOAA's recent renaming of the Gulf of Mexico to "Gulf of America" is treated as referring to the same body of water for purposes of this question.

factors.description: NHC 7-day outlook (June 5) says 'no formation expected' through ~June 12, covering most of the window
Multiple numerical models (ECMWF, GFS, Google AI) converging on Central American Gyre development in Gulf around June 13-15
ECMWF ensemble gives 35-50% probability of TD-level circulation by June 15; Dr. Maue gives >50% by June 15
Historical base rate: NHC flagged Gulf disturbances during June 8-14 in 4/11 years (36%)
2026 season predicted below-normal (El Nino developing) with enhanced wind shear
Development timing is late in window (June 13-15) so even if disturbance forms, only last 2-3 days would count

input.anchor: Historical base rate: 36% of years (4/11, 2015-2025) see NHC flag a Gulf disturbance with nonzero formation chance during June 8-14. Conditional on flagging, average ~8 TWOs count. Unconditional mean ~3.
input.assessment:
This is a zero-inflated distribution. The dominant scenario (~59%) is that nothing develops in the Gulf during June 8-14 and the answer is 0. But there's a meaningful probability (~41%) that the Central American Gyre pattern flagged by multiple models materializes, leading NHC to mention a Gulf disturbance with nonzero formation chances in the later TWOs of the window.

Key evidence:
1. CURRENT CONDITIONS: NHC says nothing for 7 days as of June 5. This is strong evidence against early-window activity (June 8-11).
2. MODEL SIGNALS: ECMWF, GFS, and Google AI models all converge on Gulf development around June 13-15 via Central American Gyre mechanism. This is a well-known early-season formation pattern.
3. TIMING: Even if development occurs, it's most likely to be flagged in the last 1-3 days of the window (June 12-14), limiting the TWO count.
4. THRESHOLD: The question requires only nonzero formation chance - NHC often flags areas with 10-20% chances several days before peak development.
5. SEASON CONTEXT: Below-normal 2026 season due to El Niño, but this mainly affects peak season (Aug-Oct), not early June.

The Monte Carlo simulation naturally produces a bimodal distribution with a large mass at 0 and a secondary cluster around 4-12 when something does develop.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor divergence: The 36% historical base rate would suggest ~64% P(0). My model gives ~59% P(0), slightly lower because the model signals push the probability up from base rate. This is a modest adjustment (+~5pp) which seems reasonable given the convergent model signals. The distribution center at 0 (median=0) reflects that the most likely single outcome is zero Gulf mentions.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. Will the Central American Gyre pattern actually materialize in the Gulf? Models at 8-10 day range have substantial uncertainty.
2. If it does materialize, will NHC flag it with nonzero formation chances before June 15, or will they wait until it's more organized?
3. The boundary between 'near the Gulf' and 'in the Gulf' - a system in the western Caribbean might or might not count depending on exact wording.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Returned excellent historical data on NHC TWOs and year-by-year analysis of Gulf activity during June 8-14. web_search: Found current weather model discussions including Dr. Maue's analysis and model probability maps. fetch_url: Successfully retrieved detailed model forecasts from clickorlando.com and saludastandard-sentinel.com. accuweather.com returned 403. execute_code: Monte Carlo simulation ran successfully for distribution construction.
input.update_triggers: 1. NHC updates their TWO to mention any Gulf disturbance (would significantly increase the count forecast). 2. Weather models lose the Gulf development signal in subsequent runs (would push toward 0). 3. A tropical wave enters the Gulf unexpectedly before June 12 (would push toward high counts).
input.process_reflection: The research() tool was extremely useful for this question - it efficiently gathered both current NHC conditions and historical frequency data. The web search turned up valuable near-real-time weather model discussions that were crucial for updating from the base rate. The Monte Carlo framework worked well for this zero-inflated distribution. One gap: I couldn't easily access archived NHC TWOs from prior years to directly count qualifying TWOs, so I had to rely on the research agent's year-by-year analysis.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Resolution criteria**: Fully recovered via get_metaculus_questions. The agent correctly parsed the resolution criteria (count of scheduled Atlantic TWOs during June 8-14 that mention a Gulf disturbance with nonzero formation chance, max 28). No resolution misalignment issues.

**Factor-by-factor verification:**

1. **Factor 1 (NHC 7-day outlook says "no formation expected")**: ✅ Confirmed. Trace shows NHC TWO at 2:00 PM EDT June 5, 2026: "Tropical cyclone formation is not expected during the next 7 days." Multiple trace sources confirm this (research tool results, direct NHC text). Logit sign (negative) is correct.

2. **Factor 2 (Multiple models converging on CAG development, June 13-15)**: ✅ Confirmed. clickorlando.com article mentions Central American Gyre pattern and "second weekend of June" (June 13-14). Saluda Standard Sentinel confirms ECMWF ensemble, Google AI FNV3 model both showing Gulf development around June 15-16. The GFS was also mentioned in a separate Saluda article. Logit sign (positive) is correct.

3. **Factor 3 (ECMWF 35-50%, Dr. Maue >50%)**: ✅ Confirmed. Saluda Standard article: "35-50% probability of tropical depression level circulation...Valid for Monday, June 15, 2026." Dr. Maue Substack: "signal for TS/Hurricane in Gulf >50% probability" for June 15. Note both cite June 15, not within the window, but the factor correctly says "by June 15." Logit sign (positive) is correct.

4. **Factor 4 (Historical base rate 4/11 years, 36%)**: ✅ Confirmed. Detailed year-by-year analysis in the research tool identifies 2015, 2017, 2020, 2021 as years with NHC nonzero Gulf formation flagging during June 8-14. 4/11 = 36%.

5. **Factor 5 (Below-normal 2026 season, El Niño)**: ✅ Confirmed. NOAA outlook: 55% below-normal, 8-14 named storms predicted. El Niño Watch with 82% chance for May-Jul 2026. Logit sign (negative) is correct.

6. **Factor 6 (Development timing late in window)**: ✅ Confirmed. Models all point to June 13-15 as the development timeframe, which is the tail end of the June 8-14 window. Logit sign (negative, limiting count) is correct.

**Potential double-counting**: Factors 2 and 3 both reference model consensus signals (ECMWF ensemble appears in both). However, Factor 2 focuses on the multi-model convergence pattern while Factor 3 focuses on specific probability quantification. Both logits are moderate (+1.5 and +1.0) and the overlap is mild, not enough to meaningfully distort the forecast.

**Contradictory assessment check**: The narrative assessment is consistent with the factors - it correctly identifies a zero-inflated distribution dominated by the ~59% probability of nothing developing (driven by NHC's current "no formation" outlook), with the upside scenario driven by model signals for late-window development. The Monte Carlo implementation faithfully translates the factor assessments.

**Counterargument quality**: Strong. The agent identifies three substantive counterpoints: (1) model signals are for June 15 (after window), with poor skill at 8-10 day range and a recent bust (May 21 models); (2) NHC's flagging threshold is higher than just model signals showing potential; (3) El Niño years systematically suppress early-season Gulf development. This is a genuine attempt at self-critique, not a token gesture.

**Probability assessment**: The center at 0 with range 0-11 (P10-P90) is reasonable. The ~59% mass at zero is well-supported by the NHC's current all-clear through ~June 12. The ~41% chance of nonzero outcome appropriately reflects the model convergence on mid-June Gulf development, tempered by the fact that the key model signals target June 15 (one day after the window closes). I considered whether P(disturbance) = 45% is slightly high since the ECMWF and Maue probabilities are for June 15, but the agent properly accounts for timing in the Monte Carlo (most flagging starts June 12-14 conditional on development), and NHC typically flags areas 1-3 days before peak development. A slightly lower P(disturbance) of ~35-40% would shift the mean from 3.2 to ~2.5-2.9, which is not a meaningful difference in the distribution shape.

**Tool audit**: Agent mentions "accuweather.com returned 403" which matches the 1 fetch_url error (20% error rate on 5 calls). No meaningful discrepancy between narrative and metrics.

**Worldview consistency**: No contradictory worldview entries found. The Houston precipitation question (43753) from June 2 noted "no organized weather systems or tropical disturbances" targeting the area, consistent with the current analysis that any Gulf development would be late in the window.
